questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring. Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding of lithium toxicity prevention?

options.text: 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.'
'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, vomiting, or excessive sweating.'
'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.'
'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.'

questions.rationale: Lithium is excreted by the kidney and competes with sodium for reabsorption. When sodium levels drop (from low-sodium diet, diarrhea, vomiting, sweating, diuretics), the kidney retains lithium — causing toxicity. Consistent sodium and fluid intake, and monitoring for dehydration causes, are essential safety measures. Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Abrupt discontinuation (C) causes rebound mania. NSAIDs like ibuprofen (D) decrease renal lithium excretion, raising lithium levels significantly — they are contraindicated.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Lithium safety: maintain consistent sodium/fluid intake; avoid NSAIDs (raise lithium levels); report vomiting/diarrhea/excessive sweating immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a 7-year-old child notices multiple bruises in various stages of healing on the torso, bilateral arm bruises with patterned marks, and the child flinches when the nurse approaches. The mother explains the bruises are from 'falling off a bike.' Which nursing action is correct?

options.text: Document the mother's explanation in the chart and continue routine care
Confront the mother and ask her directly if she is abusing the child
Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is certain
Discuss concerns with the healthcare team and only report if the physician agrees the findings are suspicious

questions.rationale: Nurses are mandatory reporters in all 50 states. Mandatory reporting requires reporting SUSPICION of child abuse — absolute certainty is not required. A nurse who fails to report suspected child abuse can face criminal charges, license sanctions, and civil liability. The threshold is reasonable suspicion based on assessment findings. The report goes directly to the state's child protective services (CPS) — this is the nurse's independent legal obligation, not contingent on physician agreement (D). Confronting the caregiver (B) may endanger the child. Documentation alone (A) does not fulfill the mandatory report obligation.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Mandatory reporting: report SUSPICION of abuse — you do not need to be certain; report directly to CPS; this is the nurse's legal obligation regardless of physician agreement.

questions.stem: A patient on a nasal cannula becomes more restless and starts speaking in short phrases. What is the most useful next step?

options.text: Reassess breathing, work of breathing, and oxygenation
Remove the oxygen because the patient is talking
Assume anxiety is the only cause
Document that the patient is stable

questions.rationale: Restlessness and short speech can be early signs of respiratory distress. The nurse should reassess the patient before assuming the device is working well. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Oxygen should not be removed reflexively. - C Anxiety may be present, but respiratory status must be checked first. - D This pattern is not enough to call the patient stable.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.

visualRationale.caption: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.
visualRationale.conclusion: Changes in speech and restlessness can be early respiratory warning signs.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient 2 days post-abdominal surgery. The patient calls the nurse and states, 'I felt something pop in my stomach and I'm looking at my intestines.' The nurse enters the room and observes evisceration of abdominal contents. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Attempt to manually replace the intestines and apply a sterile dressing
Cover the eviscerated contents with sterile normal saline-soaked gauze, do not attempt to replace them, and call for help
Place the patient supine with legs straight, apply a dry sterile dressing, and activate the surgical team
Ask the patient to lie still, apply an abdominal binder to contain the bowel, and document the finding

questions.rationale: Wound evisceration is a surgical emergency. The priority is to protect the eviscerated tissue from desiccation and contamination. Cover the bowel immediately with sterile gauze moistened with normal saline (not dry gauze, which would desiccate tissue; not replaced, which risks contamination and injury). The patient should be kept NPO, positioned to reduce tension on the wound (often supine with knees slightly flexed), and the surgical team notified immediately for emergent return to the OR. Attempting to replace bowel or using dry dressings or binders can cause irreparable harm.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Evisceration: Cover with sterile saline-soaked gauze (NEVER replace), keep patient still, call surgeon STAT, keep NPO. Dry gauze and binders are contraindicated.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a patient with severe preeclampsia on magnesium sulfate should assess for which finding FIRST in each nursing assessment?

options.text: Blood pressure and fetal heart rate
Deep tendon reflexes and respiratory rate
Urine output and temperature
Level of consciousness and oxygen saturation

questions.rationale: The first signs of magnesium toxicity are loss of deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) and respiratory rate <12/min. DTRs serve as an early warning before respiratory arrest occurs — absent DTRs (loss of patellar reflex) signal dangerously high magnesium levels and the need to stop the infusion. This assessment should occur every 1 hour during magnesium infusion. BP and FHR (A) are important but reflect preeclampsia status, not magnesium toxicity. Urine output (C) is also important (magnesium is renally excreted; oliguria leads to accumulation), but the respiratory/reflex check is the most time-critical toxicity indicator.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Magnesium monitoring priority: DTRs (patellar reflex) and respiratory rate every hour — absent DTRs signal toxicity before respiratory arrest.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
